note: Small, upright minuscule (style rectangulaire aplati ou écrasé)

note.value: Decorative bars
Previous catalogs dated this manuscript to the 13th-14th c. CE

note: Each (reconstructed) folio had 28/29 lines of text, with 8 mm between and arranged in a single column

One bifolio of Sin. gr. 966 corresponds to one original folio (61v+56r, 61r+56v), which measured approximately 255x180 mm. The Schriftspiegel is approximately 200x120 mm. The upper margin measures 15 mm (fol. 61r), the outer 40 mm (fol. 61r), the internal one 25 mm (f. 61r), and the lower 50 mm (fol. 56v)

note: Μinuscule "rectangulaire aplati ou écrasé" | Text written in an upright rectangular calligraphic minuscule with thick and pronounced strokes hanging from the ruled line. A notable peculiarity is the letter ζ "a proboscide", in the form of a three inclined to the left. This mode of writing is reminiscent of Salentine manuscripts written in "style rectangulaire aplati ou écrasé"
Each side of the bifolio preserves 22–23 lines of text, with 7 mm between and arranged in a single column
The upper margin measures 35 mm, the outer 45 mm, and the lower 35 mm (f. 85v). The original manuscript measured approximately 190x130 mm
Prickings are still visible in the outer margin of f. 80, their shape elongated rather than round, and likely made with a penknife. The ruled lines correspond to Sautel-Leroy type 24D1

note.value: Description by Ernst Gamillscheg, Giulia Rossetto
Bibliography: Rossetto 2023a
Lower (older) layer of double palimpsest
Description of undertext by Ernst Gamillscheg, Giulia Rossetto
Bibliography: Rossetto 2023a

note: The text is arranged in a single column with 30 or 31 lines per page and a distance between lines of 5 mm

The Schriftspiegel is approximately 175x90 mm. What remains of the upper margin measures 6 mm, while the outer margin measures 30 mm and the lower 20 mm (f. 101r). The original manuscript measured approximately 190x130 mm

note.value: The troparia (εἰς τὴν λειτουργίαν) are arranged according to the system of the eight modes. What is preserved goes from Saturday of tone 2 to Sunday of tone 6 (Rossetto)
Description by Ernst Gamillscheg, Giulia Rossetto
Bibliography: Rossetto 2023a
Reconstructed folio order, ff. 81, 89, 102, 95, 92, 101, 84, 96
Seven folia belong to the same quire, that can be reconstructed as follows: 81, missing folio, 89, 102, 95, 92, 101, 84 (Rossetto). Folio 96 comes from a different quire
Lower (oldest) layer of double palimpsest

Bibliography: Rossetto 2023a

note: Minuscule with abbreviations and cursive elements. Yellow wash used for highlighting titles and rubrics (e.g. on fol. 53r), indicating south Italian origin. Typical Salentine traits are also: side bands that resemble vegetal motifs (e.g. fols. 55r, 81v, 93r), omicron en rondelle, initials with double outline, asterisk with arrowheads
The undertext runs parallel to the overtext
Each side preserves between 19 and 26 lines of text, 6 mm apart and arranged in a single column

A single folio of Sin. gr. 966 corresponds to a single folio of the original manuscript, suggesting that the original manuscript was of similar dimensions: approximately 175x120 mm (oblong in height). The Schriftspiegel is approximately 135x90 mm, with margins measuring 20 mm (outer), 15 mm (inner), 30 mm (lower) and 20 mm (upper) (f. 71r)

Prickings are still visible in the margins (e.g. fol. 95), elongated in shape and likely made with a penknife. The scheme of ruled lines which support the writing corresponds to Leroy’s index X. This kind of ruling is often found in manuscripts written in Salento (Southern Italy)

note.value: Title of the manuscript (τυπικόν) on f. 71r

The text falls broadly into three parts: 1) general rules on how to celebrate services. This section occupies the first seven folia of Quire one; 2) synaxarion, listing a selection of fixed daily celebrations, from the beginning of the liturgical year on 1 September until 31 August (from the second half of Quire one to the third to last folio of Quire five); 3) triodion, with the movable cycle, running from the Sunday of the Publican and the Pharisee to Easter Monday (the last two folia of Quire five until the end of Quire seven). The final part of the manuscript—from Easter Monday to All Saints’ Sunday (the first Sunday after Pentecost)—is missing. I have calculated that approximately two quires are now lost. All in all, this means that the Typikon originally consisted of approximately 70 folia (Rossetto)
Description by Ernst Gamillscheg, Giulia Rossetto

Bibliography: G. Rossetto, Greek Palimpsests at Saint Catherine's Monastery (Sinai): Three Euchologia as Case Studies, Vienna 2022 | Typikon editions used to compare the text are: Mateos, J. Arranz SJ, Orientalia Christiana Analecta 185 (Rome, 1969) ; Papadopulos-Kerameus, A., Ἀναλέκτα Ἱεροσολυμιτικῆς Σταχυολογίας, vols. 1-4 (St. Petersburg, 1891-1898) ; Dmitrievskij, A., Opisanie liturgičeskichă rukopisej, chranjaščichsja vă bibliotekachă Pravoslavnago Vostoka, I: Τυπικά, Kiev 1895 ; Dmitrievskij, A., Opisanie liturgičeskichă rukopisej, chranjaščichsja vă bibliotekachă Pravoslavnago Vostoka, III: Τυπικά 2, Petrograd 1917
Sinai Greek NF M 21, ff. 4-7 contains parts of this same text
Upper (younger) layer of double palimpsest

Seven quires can be reconstructed. Quire 1: ff. 71, 84, 65, 92, 89, 68, 81, 78 (complete); Quire 2: ff. 95, 76, 101, 90, 91, 96, 73, 102 (complete, with quire mark on f. 95r); Quire 3: ff. 55, 70, 77, 50, 51, 72, 63, 62 (complete); Quire 4: ff. 53, 66, 100, 97, 67, 48 (quire mark on f. 53r. Third bifolio is missing); Quire 5: ff. 87, 88, 99, 64, 69, 98, 93, 94 (complete, with quire mark on f. 87r); Quire 6: ff. 83, 60, 75, 74, 57, 82 (quire mark on f. 83r. Central bifolio is missing); Quire 7: ff. 79, 54, 61, 52, 80, 85, 49, 56, 47, 86 (complete, with quire mark on f. 79r) (Rossetto)
Description of undertext by Ernst Gamillscheg, Giulia Rossetto
